content.full_text:
This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Atascadero Planning
Commissioner Thomas P. Jones for informal assistancel regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").2
Farn PolrrrcAt- PRACTICEs CouulsSIoN
428 J Street . Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
June 6, 2006
Patrick L. Enright, City Attomey
City of Atascadero
Kronick Moskovitz Tiedemann
& Girard
Attorneys At Law
4oo Capitol Mall, 27h FToor
,-Sacramento, CA 95814
RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our FiIe No. I-06-094
Dear Mr. Enright:
QUESTTONS
1. May Thomas P. Jones, a member of the city of Atascadero's planning
commission, make, participate in making, or influence decisions regarding a development
prqect with boundaries that are more than 500 feet away from the commissioner's
property even though as part of the project's mitigation process, a highway offamp
located within 500 feet of the commissioner's property will be redesigned or enlarged?
2. Would the commissioner's property be directly or indirectly involved in
decisions relating to the highway interchange of which the on-ramp is a part?
I Informal assistance does not confer the immunityprovided by a Commission opinion or formal
written advice. (Regulation 18329(c) (3), copy enclosed.)
' Gorr"rnment Code sections 81000 - 9l}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. Al1 statutory references herein are to the Government
Code unless otherwise indicated. AII regulatory references herein are to Title 2 of the Califomia Code of
Regulations unless otherwise indicated.


File No. I-06-094
Page No. 2
3. Can decisions regarding the interchange and the development project be
segmented under regulation 18702.3 so that the commissioner, while disqualified from
making, partictpating in making or influencing decisions relating to the interchange,ffi&y
nevertheless make, participate in making or influence separate decisions relating io the 
-
project?
CONCLUSIONS
1. It is presumed that arry financial effect of interchange decisions on
Commissioner Jones' real property, which is directly involved in such decisions, is
mateial, absent proof to rebut this presumption. Therefore, he has a conflict of interest
and may not participate in these decisions. With respect to decisions about the
development project itself, it is presumed that any financial effect on Commissioner
Jones' real property is not material because the outer boundaries of the project area are
more than 500 feet from his property. However, if the development ofproperty within
the project area will necessarily require redesign or enlargement of the interchange, then
the decision whether to develop the project area will also give rise to a conflict oiinterest
(see response to question no. 3 below). Finally, there is a limited exception which may
be available to Commissioner Jones that allows a public official to appear before his or
her agency, not in an official capacity, but to represent his or her personal interests.
2. Commissioner Jones' real property will be directly involved in decisions
relating to the interchange, of which the on-ramp is a part, because the on-ramp is within
500 feet ofhis real property.
3. It does not appear that the decision to develop property within the project area
can be separated from decisions to redesign or expand the interchange. Thus, if
Commissioner Jones has a disqualiffing financial interest in any decision relating to the
interchange, he will also be disqualified from participating in the decision whether to
develop property within the project area.
FACTS
The City of Atascadero's Planning Commission is considering a project
("Project") located on the west side of the Del Rio/101 Interchange (''Interclange,). The
Project will consist of a major conlmercial development and possibly other mixed uses.
The Project may also involve rezoning, a specific plan and a development agreement. In
our telephone conversations, you confirmed that as part of the Project, the planning
commission is likely to consider mitigation measures for the Interchang" *hi"h would
involve major redesign or expansion of the Interchange, which would include the off-
ramp and on-ramp on the west side of the highway.
Planning Commissioner Thomas P. Jones owns a single familyhome located on
the west side of the Interchange. It is approximately 890 feet from the nearest boundary
of the Project area. Commissioner Jones' property is approxim ately 250 feet from the


File No. I-06-094
Page No, 3
edge of the California Department of Transportation's right-of-way for the Interchange,s
southbound on-ramp.
The Atascadero City Council held a joint meeting with the planning commission
on May 30,2006, which Commissioner Jones did not attend, at which the proponent of
the Project presented conceptual plans for the property. It is anticipated that similar
meetings will be held in the future.
ANALYSIS
Ouestions I & 2.
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will .,perform
their duties in animpartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests ofpersons who have supported them." (Section g1001(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making,'or
otherwise using his orher official position to influence a govenrmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
a public official has a disquali$ring conflict of interest in a govemmental decision.
(Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists
whenever a public official makes a govemmental decision which has a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her financial interests.
Step One. Is the commissioner a o6public official" within the meaning of
section 87100?
Section 82048 defines a public official as "every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency." You have correctly identified
Commissioner Jones as a public official as a member of the city's planning commission
Step Two. By participating in planning commission decisions involving
(i) the Project or (ii) the Interchange, would Commissioner Jones be making,
participating in making or influencing a governmental decision?
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contrictual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulati on 18702.1 .) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the auihority of his or her
position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the offitial negotiates,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use h[ or her official
position to influence a goveflrmental decision if, for the purpose of influencing the


File No. I-06-094
Page No. 4
decision, the official contacts or appears before or otherwise attempts to influenc e, any
member, ofEcer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)
Your questions presuppose Commissioner Jones making or participating in making
decisions regarding the Project and the Interchange in his cipacity as, plur,rirg
commissioner. However, the facts you have provided suggest that Commissioner Jones
should also be focusing on the rule against attempting to use his "official position to
influence the decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official contacts,
or appears before or otherwise attempts to influence" any member, officer, employee, or
consultant of the planning commission. (Regulat ion I g7 02.3 .)
In addition, if Commissioner Jones attends a meeting that will include discussion
of a matter in which he has an financial interest,3 section SZIOS and regulation
18702.5(b) prohibit his silent attendance at the meeting. Section 87105 and regulation
18702.5 require that if a public official who holds an office specified in sectiorig 72004
has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public -e"ti.rg, he or she must
immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of economic
interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest on the record
of the meeting, recuse himself or herself from voting and leave the room for the duration
of the discussion and/or vote on the item
There is a narrow exception to the rule requiring the public official to leave the
room and which allows him or her to speak at the meeting. under regulation
18702-5(d)(3) (copy enclosed) a public official may speak as any othir member of the
public to advocate on behalf of his or her own personal interests.5 If Commissioner Jones
wishes to avail himself of this exception, he must nevertheless, following the
announcement of the agenda item to be discussed or voted upon but befoie either the
discussion or vote commences, publicly identifu the type of iconomic interest he holds
(an interest in real property), and give the address or another indication of the location of
the property, unless the property is his principal or personal residence, in which case,
identification that the property is a residence will suffice. If the govemmental decision is
to be made during an open session of a public meeting, his identification of his economic
interest must be made orally and shall be made part of the official public record. He must
then leave the dais to speak from the same area as members of the public. Under this
exception Commissioner Jones' comments must be limited to his pirsonal interests. and
3 Commissioner Jones has an economic interest in his real property, as discussed belowunder
Step Three.
a Planning commissioners are among the public officials specified in section g72OO.
5 Regulation ly7\2 4 defines "personal interests" to include "an interest in real property which is
wholly owned by the official or members of his or her immediate family." (Regulations itioz.41u1B1ana
(b)(1). From the facts you have provided, it appears that Commissioner Jones is the sole owner of the
property located within 500 feet of the Interchange.


File No. I-06-094
Page No. 5
he should take care to clariSr that he is not appearing in any official capacity.6 He would
still be prohibited from voting on the decisions and from privately disCussing these
matters with other members of the planning commission, city council or othir city
officials.
step Three. what are commissioner Jones, economic interests?
The economic interests from which conflicts of interest may arise are
described in section 87103 and regulations 18703-18703.5. There are six kinds of
economic interests. However, since you are only asking about the commissioner,s real
property interest, we limit our analysis to that interest. Under section 871030) of the Act
and regulation 18703.2, apttblic official has an economic interest in real prop.rty in
which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. presuma6ly,
Commissioner Jones has at least a $2,000 economic interest in his single-famiiy home.7
Step Four. WiIl Commissioner Jones' economic interest be directly or
indirectly involved in decisions he wiII make, participate in making or inliuence as a
planning commissioner?
Regulation 18704.2(a) sets forth six sets of circumstances under which real
property in which a public official has an economic interest is deerned to be directly
involved in a governmental decision, only one of which appears to apply to
Commissioner Jones in this instance.s
"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest,
or any part of that real property, is located within 500 feet
of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
property which is the subject of the govemmental decision.
For purposes of subdivision (aX5), real property is located
'within 500 feet of the boundaries (or proposed boundaries)
of the real property which is the subject of the govemmental
decision' if any part of the real property is within 500 feet
of the boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of the
redevelopment proj ect ar ea."
6 For closed sessions and consent calendars, special rules found inregulation 18702.5(c) and (d)
apply.
7 A public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal finances. (Section
87703; regulation 18703.5). However, under regulation 18705.5(a), when determining whether a
govemmental decision has a material financial effect on a public official's economic iiterest in his or her
personal finances, a financial effect on the value ofreal properly owned directly or indirectly by the official
is not considered. Thus, we limit our discussion to possiblJ financial effects on Commissioier Jones, real
properfy interests.
8 You may wish to consider the other five which are set forth in regulation lS7Oa.2@)(2)-(6).


File No. I-06-094
Page No. 6
Your account of the facts indicates govemmental decisions in two areas:
decisions relating to development of the property within the Project area anddecisions to
redesign or expand the Interchange which includes an on-ramp located less than 500 feet
from commissioner Jones' property. Under regulation 18704.2(a)(1), the
commissioner's real property would be directly involved in decisions relating to the
redesign or expansion of the Interchange because the commissioner's home is within 500
feet of the edge of the right-of-way for the Interchange's southbound on-ramp.
With respect to Project development decisions, the boundaries of the project area
are more than 500 feet from the commissioner's property and none of the other criteria
listed in regulation 18704.2(a) apply. Accordingly, his real property interest is
considered indirectly involved. When arealproperty interest is not directly involved in a
govemmental decision, it is considered to be indirectly involved. (Regulation
18704.2(d).)
Steps Five and Six. WiIl the financial effects of a decision on the public
official's economic interest be material and reasonably foreseeable?
The materiality standards applicable to Commissioner Jones' economic interest in
his real property are set forth in regulation 18705.2. The materiality standard applicable
to real property that is directly involved in a governmental decision is gorr" 
"d by
subdivision (aXl) which provides that "the financial effect of a govemmental decision on
the real property is presumed to be material. This presumption may be rebutted by proof
that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the goveflrmental decision will have any
financial effect on the real property." (Regulation 18705.2(a)(l).) This provision is
commonly referred to as the "one penny ruIe." It applies only if it is notreasonably
foreseeable that the governmental decision will have any financial effect, not even a
penny's worth, upon the official's real property.
In order to rebut the presumption of mateiality, there must be facts eonstituting
proof to support the conclusion that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the govemmental
decision will have any financial effect on a public official's property. Without proof to
overcome the presumption, decisions relating to the Interchange are presumed to be
material. You have not provided facts from which we can determine if Commissioner
Jones can rebut the presumption of materiality. The determination of whether or not it is
reasonably foreseeable that aparticular decision regarding the Project or the Interchange
will affect Commissioner Jones' property by so much as a penny's worth is necessarily a
factual question that is ultimately for him to decide. An official's mere belief that theie is
no financial effect would not be enough to rebut the presumption of materiality.
(lrlc Laughlin Advice Letter, No. A-04- I 92.)
The materiality standard applicable to real property that is indirectly involved in a
governmental decision is governed by regulation 18705.2(a)Q) which provides that ..the
financial effect of a governmental decision on real property which is indirectly involved
in the govemmental decision is presumed not to be material. This presumption maybe


File No. I-06-094
Page No. 7
rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the governmental
decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real property in which the public
official has an economic interest, which make it reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have amateial financial effect on the real property in which the official has an
interest."e
An effect is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if the effect is "substantially
likely." (Regulation 18706; In re Thorner (1975) 1 Fppc ops. 19g.) whether the
financial consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely atthetime the
decision is made depends on the specific facts surrounding the decision. 
-(In 
re Thorner,
supra.) A financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably
foreseeable. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably
foreseeable. Each decision of the planning commission involving the project must be
examined individually to determine if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a mateial financial effect on commissioner Jones' real property.
Step Seven. May the commissioner participate in a decision because the
financial effect on his interest is not distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally?
Under section 87I03 (regulation 18707) a public official who determines that his
economic interest will experience a material financial effect as a result of a governmental
decision may nevertheless participate in the decision if the financial effect o, hi. interest
is not distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. There is no suggestion in
the facts you have provided that Commissioner Jones will be participating iiltanning
commission decisions having a financial effect upon him that will not be distinguishable
from their effect on the public generally.
Step Eieht. If Commissioner Jones has a conflict of interest in a decision,
may he nevertheless participate because his participation is legally required?
Section 87101 (regulation 18703) provides an exception under which a public
official who has a conflict of interest in a governmental deCision may nevertheless
participate. There is no suggestion in the facts you have provided that Commissioner
Jones' participation in any planning commission decision involving the project or the
Interchange will be legally required.
e The regulation lists the following examples: the development potential or
income producing potential of the official's real property, the use of the olfi"iul,s real property and the
character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to, substantial effects on traffic, ,i"w, priuu"y,
intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood. For example, decisions to
develop or re-zone property within the Project area could affect the iniome producing potentiai of the
commissioner's home if a large employer or university is approved for the project area.


File No. I-06-094
Page No. 8
Ouestion 3: Segmentation of Decisions.
You have specifically asked whether decisions regarding the development project
and the Interchangemay be segmented so that if Commissioner Jones has a disqualifoing
conflict of interest with respect to one decision he may nevertheless participate in other
decisions. Generally, each govemmental decisionis analyzed independ"tly to determine
if there will be a foreseeable material financial effect on an official's financial interest.
(In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77.) The circumstances under which a public official
disqualified from one decision may participate in other related decisions are set forth in
regulation 18709:
"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public
official has a financial interest, to allow participation by the
official, provided all of the following conditions apply:
(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest
can be broken down into separate decisions that are not
inexhicably interrelated to the decision in which the official
has a disqualifying financial interest;
(2) The decision in which the offlcial has a financial interest
is segmented from the other decisions;
(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest
is considered first and a final decision is reached by the
agency without the disqualified official's participation in any
way; and
(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial
interest has been made, the disqualified public official,s
participation does not result in a reopening of or otherwise
financially affect, the decision from which the official was
disqualified.
(b) For pu{poses of this regulation, decisions are 'inextricably
interrelated' when the result of one decision will effectively
determine, affirm, nulliff, or alter the result of another decision."
(Regulation 18709(a) and (b).)
Separate decisions may be too inextricably related to be considered separately,
and, in such cases, a public official's conflict of interest in one decision will disqualify
him or her from participating in the other decisions. Since it appears that adecision to
develop property within the Project area as described in your letter will necessarily
require redesign or expansion of the Interchange, Commissioner Jones will have a
conflict of interest as to said decision because he will be unable to avail himself of the
segmentation process


File No. I-06-094
Page No. 9
That is not to say, howeve4 that segmentation may not be used with respect to
other Project decisions, provided that all of the following conditions are met: the
decisions regarding the Interchange are made first; Commissioner Jones does not make,
participate in making or attempt to influence the Interchange decisions; the decision to
develop the Project area is held only after the Interchange decisions have been made; and
Commissioner Jones will again refrain from making,participating in making or
influencing thatparticular decision. Subsequent decisions, for example those relating to
the specific details of the Project area, may then properly come befoie Commissionei
Jones so long as Commissioner Jones'participation does not result in a reopening of or
otherwise financially affect, the decisions from which he was disqualified.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division
VJ:rd

sections.question:
1. May Thomas P. Jones, a member of the city of Atascadero's planning
commission, make, participate in making, or influence decisions regarding a development
prqect with boundaries that are more than 500 feet away from the commissioner's
property even though as part of the project's mitigation process, a highway offamp
located within 500 feet of the commissioner's property will be redesigned or enlarged?
2. Would the commissioner's property be directly or indirectly involved in
decisions relating to the highway interchange of which the on-ramp is a part?
I Informal assistance does not confer the immunityprovided by a Commission opinion or formal
written advice. (Regulation 18329(c) (3), copy enclosed.)
Al1 statutory references herein are to the Government
Code unless otherwise indicated. AII regulatory references herein are to Title 2 of the Califomia Code of
Regulations unless otherwise indicated.
3. Can decisions regarding the interchange and the development project be
segmented under regulation 18702.3 so that the commissioner, while disqualified from
making, partictpating in making or influencing decisions relating to the interchange,ffi&y
nevertheless make, participate in making or influence separate decisions relating io the 
-
project?
sections.conclusion:
1. It is presumed that arry financial effect of interchange decisions on
Commissioner Jones' real property, which is directly involved in such decisions, is
mateial, absent proof to rebut this presumption. Therefore, he has a conflict of interest
and may not participate in these decisions. With respect to decisions about the
development project itself, it is presumed that any financial effect on Commissioner
Jones' real property is not material because the outer boundaries of the project area are
more than 500 feet from his property. However, if the development ofproperty within
the project area will necessarily require redesign or enlargement of the interchange, then
the decision whether to develop the project area will also give rise to a conflict oiinterest
(see response to question no. 3 below). Finally, there is a limited exception which may
be available to Commissioner Jones that allows a public official to appear before his or
her agency, not in an official capacity, but to represent his or her personal interests.
2. Commissioner Jones' real property will be directly involved in decisions
relating to the interchange, of which the on-ramp is a part, because the on-ramp is within
500 feet ofhis real property.
3. It does not appear that the decision to develop property within the project area
can be separated from decisions to redesign or expand the interchange. Thus, if
Commissioner Jones has a disqualiffing financial interest in any decision relating to the
interchange, he will also be disqualified from participating in the decision whether to
develop property within the project area.

room and which allows him or her to speak at the meeting. under regulation

public to advocate on behalf of his or her own personal interests.5 If Commissioner Jones

discussion or vote commences, publicly identifu the type of iconomic interest he holds

the property, unless the property is his principal or personal residence, in which case,

interest must be made orally and shall be made part of the official public record. He must
then leave the dais to speak from the same area as members of the public. Under this
exception Commissioner Jones' comments must be limited to his pirsonal interests. and

a Planning commissioners are among the public officials specified in section g72OO.

or any part of that real property, is located within 500 feet
of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
property which is the subject of the govemmental decision.
For purposes of subdivision (aX5), real property is located
'within 500 feet of the boundaries (or proposed boundaries)
of the real property which is the subject of the govemmental
decision' if any part of the real property is within 500 feet
of the boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of the
redevelopment proj ect ar ea."
6 For closed sessions and consent calendars, special rules found inregulation 18702.5(c) and (d)
apply.
7 A public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal finances. (Section
87703; regulation 18703.5). However, under regulation 18705.5(a), when determining whether a
govemmental decision has a material financial effect on a public official's economic iiterest in his or her
personal finances, a financial effect on the value ofreal properly owned directly or indirectly by the official
is not considered. Thus, we limit our discussion to possiblJ financial effects on Commissioier Jones, real
properfy interests.
8 You may wish to consider the other five which are set forth in regulation lS7Oa.2@)(2)-(6).
Your account of the facts indicates govemmental decisions in two areas:
decisions relating to development of the property within the Project area anddecisions to
redesign or expand the Interchange which includes an on-ramp located less than 500 feet
from commissioner Jones' property. Under regulation 18704.2(a)(1), the
commissioner's real property would be directly involved in decisions relating to the
redesign or expansion of the Interchange because the commissioner's home is within 500
feet of the edge of the right-of-way for the Interchange's southbound on-ramp.
With respect to Project development decisions, the boundaries of the project area
are more than 500 feet from the commissioner's property and none of the other criteria
listed in regulation 18704.2(a) apply. Accordingly, his real property interest is
considered indirectly involved. When arealproperty interest is not directly involved in a
govemmental decision, it is considered to be indirectly involved. (Regulation
18704.2(d).)
Steps Five and Six. WiIl the financial effects of a decision on the public
official's economic interest be material and reasonably foreseeable?
The materiality standards applicable to Commissioner Jones' economic interest in
his real property are set forth in regulation 18705.2. The materiality standard applicable
to real property that is directly involved in a governmental decision is gorr" 
"d by
subdivision (aXl) which provides that "the financial effect of a govemmental decision on
the real property is presumed to be material. This presumption may be rebutted by proof
that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the goveflrmental decision will have any
financial effect on the real property." (Regulation 18705.2(a)(l).) This provision is
commonly referred to as the "one penny ruIe." It applies only if it is notreasonably
foreseeable that the governmental decision will have any financial effect, not even a
penny's worth, upon the official's real property.
In order to rebut the presumption of mateiality, there must be facts eonstituting
proof to support the conclusion that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the govemmental
decision will have any financial effect on a public official's property. Without proof to
overcome the presumption, decisions relating to the Interchange are presumed to be
material. You have not provided facts from which we can determine if Commissioner
Jones can rebut the presumption of materiality. The determination of whether or not it is
reasonably foreseeable that aparticular decision regarding the Project or the Interchange
will affect Commissioner Jones' property by so much as a penny's worth is necessarily a
factual question that is ultimately for him to decide. An official's mere belief that theie is
no financial effect would not be enough to rebut the presumption of materiality.
(lrlc Laughlin Advice Letter, No. A-04- I 92.)
The materiality standard applicable to real property that is indirectly involved in a
governmental decision is governed by regulation 18705.2(a)Q) which provides that ..the
financial effect of a governmental decision on real property which is indirectly involved
in the govemmental decision is presumed not to be material. This presumption maybe
rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the governmental
decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real property in which the public
official has an economic interest, which make it reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have amateial financial effect on the real property in which the official has an
interest."e
An effect is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if the effect is "substantially
likely." (Regulation 18706; In re Thorner (1975) 1 Fppc ops. 19g.) whether the
financial consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely atthetime the
decision is made depends on the specific facts surrounding the decision. 
-(In 
re Thorner,
supra.) A financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably
foreseeable. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably
foreseeable. Each decision of the planning commission involving the project must be
examined individually to determine if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a mateial financial effect on commissioner Jones' real property.
Step Seven. May the commissioner participate in a decision because the
financial effect on his interest is not distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally?
Under section 87I03 (regulation 18707) a public official who determines that his
economic interest will experience a material financial effect as a result of a governmental
decision may nevertheless participate in the decision if the financial effect o, hi. interest
is not distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. There is no suggestion in
the facts you have provided that Commissioner Jones will be participating iiltanning
commission decisions having a financial effect upon him that will not be distinguishable
from their effect on the public generally.
Step Eieht. If Commissioner Jones has a conflict of interest in a decision,
may he nevertheless participate because his participation is legally required?
Section 87101 (regulation 18703) provides an exception under which a public
official who has a conflict of interest in a governmental deCision may nevertheless
participate. There is no suggestion in the facts you have provided that Commissioner
Jones' participation in any planning commission decision involving the project or the
Interchange will be legally required.
e The regulation lists the following examples: the development potential or
income producing potential of the official's real property, the use of the olfi"iul,s real property and the
character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to, substantial effects on traffic, ,i"w, priuu"y,
intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood. For example, decisions to
develop or re-zone property within the Project area could affect the iniome producing potentiai of the
commissioner's home if a large employer or university is approved for the project area.
Ouestion 3: Segmentation of Decisions.
You have specifically asked whether decisions regarding the development project
and the Interchangemay be segmented so that if Commissioner Jones has a disqualifoing
conflict of interest with respect to one decision he may nevertheless participate in other
decisions. Generally, each govemmental decisionis analyzed independ"tly to determine
if there will be a foreseeable material financial effect on an official's financial interest.
(In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77.) The circumstances under which a public official
disqualified from one decision may participate in other related decisions are set forth in
regulation 18709:
"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public
official has a financial interest, to allow participation by the
official, provided all of the following conditions apply:
(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest
can be broken down into separate decisions that are not
inexhicably interrelated to the decision in which the official
has a disqualifying financial interest;
(2) The decision in which the offlcial has a financial interest
is segmented from the other decisions;
(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest
is considered first and a final decision is reached by the
agency without the disqualified official's participation in any
way; and
(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial
interest has been made, the disqualified public official,s
participation does not result in a reopening of or otherwise
financially affect, the decision from which the official was
disqualified.
(b) For pu{poses of this regulation, decisions are 'inextricably
interrelated' when the result of one decision will effectively
determine, affirm, nulliff, or alter the result of another decision."
(Regulation 18709(a) and (b).)
Separate decisions may be too inextricably related to be considered separately,
and, in such cases, a public official's conflict of interest in one decision will disqualify
him or her from participating in the other decisions. Since it appears that adecision to
develop property within the Project area as described in your letter will necessarily
require redesign or expansion of the Interchange, Commissioner Jones will have a
conflict of interest as to said decision because he will be unable to avail himself of the
segmentation process
That is not to say, howeve4 that segmentation may not be used with respect to
other Project decisions, provided that all of the following conditions are met: the
decisions regarding the Interchange are made first; Commissioner Jones does not make,
participate in making or attempt to influence the Interchange decisions; the decision to
develop the Project area is held only after the Interchange decisions have been made; and
Commissioner Jones will again refrain from making,participating in making or
influencing thatparticular decision. Subsequent decisions, for example those relating to
the specific details of the Project area, may then properly come befoie Commissionei
Jones so long as Commissioner Jones'participation does not result in a reopening of or
otherwise financially affect, the decisions from which he was disqualified.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. May Thomas P. Jones, a member of the city of Atascadero's planning
commission, make, participate in making, or influence decisions regarding a development
prqect with boundaries that are more than 500 feet away from the commissioner's
property even though as part of the project's mitigation process, a highway offamp
located within 500 feet of the commissioner's property will be redesigned or enlarged?
2. Would the commissioner's property be directly or indirectly involved in
decisions relating to the highway interchange of which the on-ramp is a part?
I Informal assistance does not confer the immunityprovided by a Commission opinion or formal
written advice. (Regulation 18329(c) (3), copy enclosed.)
Al1 statutory references herein are to the Government
Code unless otherwise indicated. AII regulatory references herein are to Title 2 of the Califomia Code of
Regulations unless otherwise indicated.
3. Can decisions regarding the interchange and the development project be
segmented under regulation 18702.3 so that the commissioner, while disqualified from
making, partictpating in making or influencing decisions relating to the interchange,ffi&y
nevertheless make, participate in making or influence separate decisions relating io the 
-
project?

CONCLUSION: 1. It is presumed that arry financial effect of interchange decisions on
Commissioner Jones' real property, which is directly involved in such decisions, is
mateial, absent proof to rebut this presumption. Therefore, he has a conflict of interest
and may not participate in these decisions. With respect to decisions about the
development project itself, it is presumed that any financial effect on Commissioner
Jones' real property is not material because the outer boundaries of the project area are
more than 500 feet from his property. However, if the development ofproperty within
the project area will necessarily require redesign or enlargement of the interchange, then
the decision whether to develop the project area will also give rise to a conflict oiinterest
(see response to question no. 3 below). Finally, there is a limited exception which may
be available to Commissioner Jones that allows a public official to appear before his or
her agency, not in an official capacity, but to represent his or her personal interests.
2. Commissioner Jones' real property will be directly involved in decisions
relating to the interchange, of which the on-ramp is a part, because the on-ramp is within
500 feet ofhis real property.
3. It does not appear that the decision to develop property within the project area
can be separated from decisions to redesign or expand the interchange. Thus, if
Commissioner Jones has a disqualiffing financial interest in any decision relating to the
interchange, he will also be disqualified from participating in the decision whether to
develop property within the project area.
